summary: Gruber pushes back against the narrative that EU iPhone users are having more fun thanks to DMA regulations. He responds to articles by Allison Johnson (The Verge) and Federico Viticci (MacStories) celebrating the EU's expanded iPhone freedoms — third-party app stores, browser engine choice, default app replacement — by arguing the actual software available exclusively in the EU amounts to little more than Fortnite. Meanwhile, EU users are locked out of Apple Intelligence and iPhone Mirroring, features Gruber considers genuinely useful and fun. He uses a Casino analogy to frame the EU side as the party insisting they're having a good time while sitting alone in a booth.
key_points: The DMA-exclusive software ecosystem in the EU is currently thin — essentially just Fortnite and some piracy-adjacent apps, with no compelling third-party app stores or browser engines materializing yet
EU users are losing access to Apple Intelligence and iPhone Mirroring because Apple is withholding features over regulatory uncertainty, creating a real fun deficit
Gruber is skeptical of the claimed benefits of third-party browser engines on iOS, pointing to Chrome's battery drain and Google's incognito mode privacy violations
The Casino analogy frames the debate: the EU side claims to be having more fun, but the evidence suggests otherwise
Gruber challenges the framing that regulatory flexibility automatically equals a better user experience, arguing fun should be measured by what people actually use, not theoretical possibilities
money_quotes: Imagine if Chrome could deplete your iPhone battery as fast as it does your MacBook battery.

There are a bunch of articles informing EU citizens how to access Apple Intelligence by lying about where they are. There are crickets chirping regarding how iOS users outside the EU can cheat their way into the EU's new DMA rules. No one cares.
Should we judge how much fun each side of the continental divide is having by how much fun they theoretically could be having, or by how much fun they are having?
The non-Epic iOS software available exclusively in the EU is ... well, nothing of interest. Maybe some apps that help with content piracy. Other than that, nothing.
As it stands, the fun side is not the EU. But hope springs eternal.
Santoro, who is absolutely not having a good time, replies, 'So are we.' I've been thinking about that scene a lot, lately.

On the EU side, there is Fortnite and other games from Epic, a shady company that was justifiably booted from the App Store for bait-and-switch chicanery intended to provoke a lawsuit in which they got their asses handed back to them.
key_insight: The EU's DMA-mandated iPhone freedoms are celebrated in theory, but measured by what users actually do and what software actually exists, the non-EU iPhone experience with Apple Intelligence and iPhone Mirroring is currently more compelling.
